title: Two Pointers and Sliding Window
description: Core pattern deck for problems that need left-right movement instead of nested loops.

cards.question: When should I think about the two pointers pattern?
cards.shortAnswer: When I can solve the problem by moving through the data from two positions instead of restarting scans.
cards.expectedAnswer: I think about two pointers when the input is ordered, or when I need to compare, shrink, or grow a range without redoing work. It often appears in array and string problems where nested loops would be too expensive. The pattern works because each pointer usually moves forward only a limited number of times.
keyPoints: helps avoid repeated scans or nested loops
common when input is sorted or window boundaries matter
commonTraps: forcing two pointers on a problem that really needs a hashmap
followUps: What changes when the array is sorted?
How is this different from sliding window?

cards.question: When should I think about sliding window?
cards.shortAnswer: When the problem asks about a contiguous subarray or substring and I can update the answer as the window moves.
cards.expectedAnswer: Sliding window is a strong hint when the problem asks for the longest, shortest, or valid contiguous subarray or substring. Instead of recalculating from scratch, I maintain a window and update it as I move right and sometimes shrink from the left. The big gain is that I reuse previous work instead of restarting every time.
keyPoints: maintain and update a window incrementally
commonTraps: using sliding window on non-contiguous selection problems
not knowing what condition makes the window valid
followUps: When do you shrink the left side?
What extra structure is often used with sliding window?
